content:
# Leviticus Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67r–67v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_015_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_015_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 101–102 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 15 is the fourth and final chapter of the major purity-impurity legislation block of chs.11–15. Where chs.11–12 addressed animals and childbirth, and chs.13–14 addressed tzara'at, ch.15 addresses genital discharges. The chapter is formally addressed to both Moshe and Aharon (v.1), as was ch.13, and concludes with a purpose-summary (vv.31–33) parallel to the colophon of ch.14. The chapter divides into four carefully parallel units, structured by gender and by the distinction between abnormal and normal discharge: (1) vv.2–15: the male with abnormal discharge (zav [זָב], the man with zov [זוֹב]); (2) vv.16–18: the male with normal seminal emission (shkhvat-zera [שִׁכְבַת-זֶרַע]); (3) vv.19–24: the female with normal menstrual flow (niddah [נִדָּה]); (4) vv.25–30: the female with abnormal discharge (zavah [זָבָה]). Sections 1 and 4 (abnormal discharges) require the same eight-day sequence ending with two birds before the priest; sections 2 and 3 (normal discharges) require only bathing and laundering until evening or seven days. The chapter reveals a theology of the body: the boundaries of the body — particularly the generative organs — are sites of concern for the holy. The discharge regulations are not about hygiene or sin per se, but about the management of powerful life-forces (seed, blood) that, when uncontained, transmit a tum'ah requiring ritual resolution before approach to the sanctuary.]*

---

**1** And the LORD [YHWH] spoke [vayedabber] to Moshe and to Aharon [el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon] [el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon — "to Moshe and to Aharon"; the dual address, as in 13:1 and 14:33; the legislation requires both the civil-legislative authority (Moshe) and the priestly-ritual authority (Aharon) because it governs both community order and sanctuary access; the LXX renders: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων (Kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōysēn kai Aarōn legōn, "And the LORD spoke to Moshe and to Aharon saying")] saying [lemor]:

**2** "Speak [dabberu] to the sons of Yisra'el [el-benei Yisra'el] [el-benei Yisra'el — "to the sons of Israel"; the legislation applies to the entire community, not only priests; genital-discharge impurity can befall any member of the community; LXX: λαλήσατε τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ (lalēsate tois huiois Israēl)] and say to them [ve-amartem alehem]: Any man [ish ish] [ish ish — "a man, a man / any man"; the doubled indefinite ish ish is an idiom of generalization, equivalent to "whoever" or "any man whatsoever"; it is inclusive: no male is excepted from this legislation; LXX: Ἄνθρωπος ἄνθρωπος (Anthrōpos anthrōpos, "any man whatsoever")] who has a discharge [ki yihyeh zav] [ki yihyeh zav — "who has / if there is a man who is a zav"; yihyeh = "there is / there shall be"; zav [זָב] = the technical term for a man with an abnormal genital discharge; the root is zuv ("to flow, to discharge, to run"); the zav is defined by his condition: the man who flows/discharges; LXX: ὃς ἐὰν ῥύσιν ῥέῃ (hos ean rhysin rhēē, "who flows a flow"), using ῥύσις (rhysis, "flow, flux, discharge") — from ῥέω (rheō, "to flow")] from his flesh [mi-besaro] [mi-besaro — "from his flesh / from his body"; besaro = "his flesh, his body"; the discharge flows from his flesh/genitalia; LXX: ἐκ τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ (ek tou sōmatos autou, "from his body")] — his discharge [zo'vo] [zo'vo — "his discharge / his flow"; zov [זוֹב] is the noun form of the root zuv ("to flow"): the discharge itself; LXX: ἡ ῥύσις αὐτοῦ (hē rhysis autou, "his discharge")] is unclean [tameh hu'] [tameh hu' — "is unclean / he is unclean"; the nominal verdict; the condition of zov renders the man tameh; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται (akathartos estai, "he shall be unclean")]:

**3** "And this [ve-zot] shall be his uncleanness [tum'ato] in his discharge [be-zo'vo] [be-zo'vo — "in his discharge / because of his discharge"; the governing phrase; the tum'ah is defined by and tied to the condition of zov; LXX: καὶ αὕτη ἡ ἀκαθαρσία τῆς ῥύσεως αὐτοῦ (kai hautē hē akatharsia tēs rhyseōs autou, "and this is the uncleanness of his discharge")] — whether his flesh [besaro] runs [rur] [rur — "flows / drips"; qal infinitive absolute of rir or ruv — the verb of flowing or dripping; this infinitive absolute functions here as a governing condition: "whether his flesh flows with his discharge or his flesh has retained/stopped from his discharge"; LXX: εἴτε ῥεῖ (eite rhei, "whether it flows")] with his discharge [et-zo'vo] [et-zo'vo — "his discharge"; the ongoing, flowing condition] or his flesh [besaro] has retained [hechtim] [hechtim — "has retained / has become stopped/sealed"; the hiphil of chatam ("to seal, to stop up, to block"): "has caused to be sealed up, has retained its discharge"; this is the paradox of the verse: the man is tameh both when the discharge is actively flowing (rur) AND when it is stopped/retained (hechtim); both the active flow and the cessation of flow while the condition persists render him tameh; LXX: εἴτε ἐπιλέλοιπεν (eite epileloipen, "or has ceased/left off"), using ἐπιλείπω (epileipō, "to leave off, to cease, to fail") — rendering hechtim as cessation rather than retention; the LXX here understands the verse as covering two temporal states of the condition] from his discharge [mi-zo'vo] — his uncleanness [tum'ato] it is [hi'] [tum'ato hi' — "it is his uncleanness"; the tum'ah is established in either state]:

**4** "Every [kol] bed [hamishkav] [hamishkav — "the bed / sleeping-surface"; mishkav from shakav ("to lie down"): the surface on which one sleeps or reclines; LXX: πᾶσα κοίτη (pasa koitē, "every bed/couch"), using κοίτη (koitē, "bed, couch, resting-place")] upon which the zav lies [yishkav alav ha-zav] [yishkav alav ha-zav — "upon which the zav lies"; the zav as grammatical subject, the bed as object of his lying; LXX: ἐφ' ἧς ἂν κοιμηθῇ ὁ γονορρυής (eph' hēs an koimēthē ho gonorrhyēs, "upon which the one with gonorrhoea shall sleep"), using γονορρυής (gonorrhyēs, "one with a flow, one with gonorrhoea") — the LXX term for zav] shall be unclean [yitma] [yitma — "shall be unclean"; qal imperfect of tame', "to become/be unclean"; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται (akathartos estai, "shall be unclean")] — and every [ve-khol] implement [ha-keli] [ha-keli — "the implement / the vessel / the object"; keli = any object, article, or vessel; LXX: πᾶν σκεῦος (pan skeuos, "every vessel/implement")] upon which he sits [yeshev alav] [yeshev alav — "he sits upon it"; yashav ("to sit, to dwell"): the zav's sitting on any object conveys tum'ah; LXX: ἐφ' οὗ ἂν καθίσῃ (eph' hou an kathisē, "upon which he shall sit")] shall be unclean [yitma]:

**5** "And the man [ve-ish] who touches [yigga'] his bed [be-mishkavo] [be-mishkavo — "his bed"; the zav's sleeping-surface; LXX: ὁ ἁπτόμενος τῆς κοίτης αὐτοῦ (ho haptomenos tēs koitēs autou, "the one touching his bed")] shall wash his clothes [yekabbes begadav] [yekabbes begadav — "shall launder his garments"; kibbes piel: to launder thoroughly; LXX: πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ (plynei ta himatia autou, "shall wash his garments")] and shall bathe in water [ve-rachatz ba-mayim] [ve-rachatz ba-mayim — "and shall bathe in water"; rachatz qal: the bodily immersion; LXX: καὶ λούσεται ὕδατι (kai loussetai hydati, "and shall bathe in water")] and shall be unclean [ve-tame] until the evening [ad ha-erev] [ad ha-erev — "until the evening"; the standard duration for contact-tum'ah; LXX: καὶ ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται ἕως ἑσπέρας (kai akathartos estai heōs hesperas, "and shall be unclean until evening")]:

**6** "And the one who sits [ve-ha-yoshev] on the implement [al-ha-keli] upon which [asher-yeshev alav] the zav has sat [ha-zav] [ha-zav — the zav's sitting has already contaminated the implement; now a second person sits on that already-contaminated object; LXX: καὶ ὁ καθήμενος ἐπὶ τοῦ σκεύους οὗ ἂν καθίσῃ ἐπ' αὐτοῦ ὁ γονορρυής (kai ho kathēmenos epi tou skeuos hou an kathisē ep' autou ho gonorrhyēs, "and the one sitting on the implement upon which the zav sat")] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water [ve-kibbes begadav ve-rachatz ba-mayim] and shall be unclean until the evening [ve-tame ad ha-erev] [the laundering-bathing-until-evening formula is repeated identically for each contact scenario in this section; it will not be re-glossed at every repetition]:

**7** "And the one who touches [ve-ha-nogea'] the flesh [bi-vesar] of the zav [ha-zav] [bi-vesar ha-zav — "the flesh of the zav"; direct bodily contact with the zav's own body, not just his objects; LXX: ὁ ἁπτόμενος τοῦ χρωτὸς τοῦ γονορρυοῦς (ho haptomenos tou chrōtos tou gonorrhyous, "the one touching the skin of the zav"), using χρώς (chrōs, "skin, complexion") for basar ("flesh")] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**8** "And if the zav spits [ki-yirqoq ha-zav] [ki-yirqoq ha-zav — "if the zav spits"; raqaq qal: "to spit"; the act of spitting is a mechanism of transmission; the zav's saliva conveys tum'ah to any clean person it contacts; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ πτύσῃ ὁ γονορρυής (ean de ptysē ho gonorrhyēs, "if the zav spits"), using πτύω (ptyō, "to spit")] upon a clean person [ba-tahor] [ba-tahor — "upon the clean one / upon a ritually clean person"; tahor as noun: the clean person is the recipient; LXX: ἐπὶ τὸν καθαρόν (epi ton katharon, "upon the clean one")] — he [the clean person] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**9** "And every [ve-khol] riding-implement [ha-merkav] [ha-merkav — "the saddle / riding-implement"; merkav from rakhav ("to ride"): anything ridden upon — a saddle, a mount's back-covering, a riding-seat; LXX: πᾶν ἐπίσαγμα (pan episagma, "every riding-pack, saddle"), using ἐπίσαγμα (episagma, "saddle, pack-saddle, cushion for riding") — the standard LXX rendering of merkav] upon which [asher] the zav rides [yirkav alav ha-zav] [yirkav alav ha-zav — "upon which the zav rides"; riding conveys tum'ah to the riding-implement; a broader category than the bed (mishkav) and sitting-implement (keli yoshev) of vv.4–6: here the conveyance is the infected item; LXX: ἐφ' οὗ ἂν ἐπιβῇ ὁ γονορρυής (eph' hou an epibē ho gonorrhyēs, "upon which the zav shall mount")] shall be unclean [yitma]:

**10** "And everyone [ve-khol] who touches [ha-nogea'] anything [be-khol] that was under him [asher yihyeh tachtav] [asher yihyeh tachtav — "which was beneath him / which was under him"; tachat = "beneath, under, in the place of"; any object that has been under the zav — beneath his body — is tameh, and touching such objects conveys a lighter tum'ah; LXX: πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος ὅσα ἐὰν ᾖ ὑποκάτω αὐτοῦ (pas ho haptomenos hosa ean ē hypokatō autou, "all who touch whatever was beneath him")] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma ad ha-erev] — and the one who carries [ve-ha-nos'e] them [otam] [ve-ha-nos'e otam — "and the one carrying them"; nasa' qal participle: "the carrier, the one who bears"; carrying the objects that were under the zav conveys tum'ah, not just touching them; the carrier must launder; LXX: καὶ ὁ αἴρων αὐτά (kai ho airōn auta, "and the one lifting/carrying them")] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**11** "And everyone [ve-khol] whom the zav touches [asher yigga'-bo ha-zav] [asher yigga'-bo ha-zav — "whom the zav touches / whom the zav has touched with reference to"; bo = "in him / on him"; the zav actively touching a clean person; LXX: ὧν ἂν ἅψηται ὁ γονορρυής (hōn an hapsētai ho gonorrhyēs, "whom the zav touches")] without [ve-yadav] rinsing his hands [lo' shafal be-mayim] [ve-yadav lo' shafal be-mayim — "and his hands he has not rinsed in water"; shafal = to rinse, to immerse (from shafat? — likely shataph, "to rinse, to flood, to overflow"); the clause is conditional: if the zav has not rinsed his hands beforehand, his touch conveys tum'ah; it implies that if the zav HAD rinsed his hands he would not transmit through touch — a form of hygiene-ritual mitigating transmission; LXX: καὶ αἱ χεῖρες αὐτοῦ οὐ νένιπται ὕδατι (kai hai cheires autou ou neniptai hydati, "and his hands have not been washed in water"), using νίπτω (niptō, "to wash, to rinse") — the term for hand-washing in Greek] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**12** "And an earthen vessel [u-khli-cheres] [u-khli-cheres — "and an earthen vessel / a clay pot"; keli cheres as in ch.14; LXX: καὶ σκεῦος ὀστράκινον (kai skeuos ostrakinon, "and an earthen vessel")] that the zav touches [asher yigga'-bo ha-zav] shall be broken [yishaver] [yishaver — "shall be broken / shall be smashed"; niphal of shavar ("to break, to smash"): passive, "shall be broken" — the imperative is indirect; the earthen vessel cannot be de-contaminated by washing (unlike metal or wooden vessels) and so must be destroyed; LXX: ὃ ἐὰν ἅψηται αὐτοῦ ὁ γονορρυής συντριβήσεται (ho ean hapsētai autou ho gonorrhyēs syntribēsetai, "which the zav touches shall be shattered")] — and every wooden vessel [ve-khol-keli etz] [ve-khol-keli etz — "and every wooden implement"; keli etz = "wooden vessel / wooden object"; wood, unlike clay, CAN be purified; LXX: καὶ πᾶν σκεῦος ξύλινον (kai pan skeuos xylion, "and every wooden vessel")] shall be rinsed [yishatef] [yishatef — "shall be rinsed / shall be washed through"; niphal of shataph ("to rinse, to wash out, to flood"): passive: "shall be rinsed"; shataph implies a flowing-water rinse, distinct from the immersion-bathing (rachatz) of the person; LXX: ἀποπλυθήσεται (apoplotēsetai, "shall be washed off")] in water [ba-mayim]:

**13** "And when the zav [ki-yihar ha-zav] is cleansed [mi-zo'vo] from his discharge [ve-safar lo] [ve-safar lo — "and he shall count for himself"; safar qal: "to count, to number"; the zav counts seven days after the cessation of the discharge, not at the first clean day but after seven clean days have passed; LXX: καὶ ἐξαριθμήσει (kai exarithmēsei, "and shall count"), using ἐξαριθμέω (exarithmēō, "to number, to count")] seven days [shivat yamim] for his cleansing [le-taharat'o] [le-taharat'o — "for his purification / for his being declared clean"; the sevenfold waiting period, as in tzara'at purification; LXX: ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας εἰς τὴν κάθαρσιν αὐτοῦ (hepta hēmeras eis tēn katharsin autou, "seven days for his purification")] — and he shall wash [ve-kibbes] his clothes [begadav] and shall bathe [ve-rachatz] his flesh [besaro] in living water [be-mayim chayyim] [be-mayim chayyim — "in living water"; fresh running water, as in ch.14; the bathing must be in mayim chayyim specifically; LXX: καὶ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ λούσεται τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ ἐν ὕδατι ζῶντι (kai plynei ta himatia autou kai loussetai to sōma autou en hydati zōnti, "and shall wash his garments and shall bathe his body in living water")] and shall be clean [ve-taher]:

**14** "And on the eighth day [u-va-yom ha-shemini] [u-va-yom ha-shemini — "and on the eighth day"; the same eighth-day offering structure as the tzara'at restoration of 14:10; the eighth-day follows the seven-day clean period; LXX: καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ (kai tē hēmera tē ogdoē, "and on the eighth day")] he shall take [yiqqach-lo] for himself [lo] [yiqqach-lo — "shall take for himself"; the reflexive lo added: the zav procures the offerings for himself; LXX: λήμψεται ἑαυτῷ (lēmpsetai heautō, "shall take for himself")] two turtledoves [shtei torim] [shtei torim — "two turtledoves"; tor pl. torim; the two-bird offering is the affordable default, not the luxury option as in tzara'at — indicating the discharge legislation uses a different offering scale throughout; LXX: δύο τρυγόνας (dyo trygones, "two turtledoves")] or two young pigeons [o shnei benei yonah] [o shnei benei yonah — "or two young doves"; the alternative; LXX: ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν (ē dyo neossous peristerōn, "or two young pigeons")] and he shall come [u-va] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] to the entrance [el-petach] of the tent of meeting [ohel mo'ed] [ohel mo'ed — "tent of meeting"; the portable sanctuary; LXX: καὶ ἥξει ἔναντι κυρίου ἐπὶ τὴν θύραν τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου (kai hēxei enanti kyriou epi tēn thyran tēs skēnēs tou martyriou, "and shall come before the LORD at the door of the tent of testimony")] and shall give them [ve-natan otam] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**15** "And the priest shall make them [ve-'asah otam ha-kohen] [ve-'asah otam ha-kohen — "and the priest shall make/prepare/offer them"; 'asah in the cultic sense, "to perform, to make (an offering)"; LXX: καὶ ποιήσει αὐτά ὁ ἱερεύς (kai poiēsei auta ho hiereus, "and the priest shall make/offer them")] — one [ha-echad] as a sin offering [chattat] [chattat — "sin offering"; the chattat always precedes the olah in the offering sequence; LXX: τὸ ἓν περὶ ἁμαρτίας (to hen peri hamartias, "the one as a sin offering")] and one [ha-echad] as a burnt offering [olah] [olah — "burnt offering"; LXX: καὶ τὸ ἓν εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα (kai to hen eis holokautōma, "and the one as a burnt offering")] — and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khiper alav ha-kohen] [ve-khiper alav ha-kohen — "and the priest shall make atonement over/for him"; kipper piel as throughout Leviticus: the central atonement-act; LXX: καὶ ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτοῦ ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exilasetai peri autou ho hiereus, "and the priest shall make expiation for him")] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] for his discharge [me-zo'vo] [me-zo'vo — "on account of his discharge / from his discharge"; the atonement is specifically designated for the condition of zov; LXX: ἀπὸ τῆς ῥύσεως αὐτοῦ (apo tēs rhyseōs autou, "from his discharge")]:

---

**16** "And a man [ve-ish] from whom there goes out [ki-teze] [ki-teze — "when there goes out from him"; yatza' qal 3fs (discharge as subject): "when it goes out / when it issues forth"; LXX: ἐξ οὗ ἂν ἐξέλθῃ (ex hou an exelthē, "from whom goes out")] an emission of seed [shkhvat-zera] [shkhvat-zera [שִׁכְבַת-זֶרַע] — "lying of seed / emission of seed"; shkhvat = construct of mishkav ("lying, bed") — related to shakav ("to lie"); zera = "seed, semen"; the term shkhvat-zera is the Hebrew technical term for seminal emission; this verse opens the second section: the normal seminal emission (as opposed to the pathological zov of vv.2–15); LXX: σπέρματος κοίτη (spermatos koitē, "a lying/emission of seed"), using σπέρμα (sperma, "seed, semen")] — he shall bathe [ve-rachatz] his entire flesh [et-kol-besaro] [et-kol-besaro — "all his flesh / his entire body"; kol besaro = the whole body, not partial washing; LXX: λούσεται πᾶν τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ (loussetai pan to sōma autou, "shall bathe his entire body")] in water [ba-mayim] and shall be unclean [ve-tame] until the evening [ad ha-erev]:

**17** "And every garment [ve-khol-beged] [ve-khol-beged — "and every garment"; LXX: καὶ πᾶν ἱμάτιον (kai pan himation, "and every garment")] and every skin [ve-khol-or] [ve-khol-or — "and every skin / every leather object"; or = "skin, hide, leather"; LXX: καὶ πᾶν δέρμα (kai pan derma, "and every hide/skin")] upon which [asher-yihyeh alav] there shall be [shkhvat-zera] an emission of seed [shkhvat-zera] [shkhvat-zera — the seminal emission that has come into contact with the garment or leather; LXX: ἐφ' ᾧ ἐὰν ᾖ σπέρματος κοίτη (eph' hō ean ē spermatos koitē, "upon which there shall be an emission of seed")] — it shall be washed [ve-kubbes] [ve-kubbes — "shall be washed / laundered"; pual passive of kabbes ("to launder"): "it shall be laundered"; LXX: πλυθήσεται ὕδατι (plythēsetai hydati, "shall be washed in water")] in water [ba-mayim] and shall be unclean [ve-tame] until the evening:

**18** "And a woman [ve-ishah] with whom [asher] a man [ish] lies [yishkav] with an emission of seed [shkhvat-zera] [shkhvat-zera — the emission of seed in the context of sexual intercourse; this verse addresses the female partner in the act; LXX: γυνὴ ἥτις ἐὰν κοιμηθῇ ἀνὴρ μετ' αὐτῆς κοίτῃ σπέρματος (gynē hētis ean koimēthē anēr met' autēs koitē spermatos, "a woman with whom a man lies in an emission of seed")] — they [u-rachtzu] shall both bathe [shneihem] [u-rachtzu shneihem — "and they shall both bathe"; the dual bathing requirement: both partners must bathe; shneihem = "both of them, the two of them"; LXX: λούσονται ὕδατι (lousontai hydati, "shall bathe in water") — the LXX does not explicitly state "both of them" (shneihem) but the plural covers both; MT is more explicit] in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

---

**19** "And a woman [ve-ishah] — when her discharge [ki-tihyeh zo'vah] [ki-tihyeh zo'vah — "when she has / when she is discharging"; zo'vah is the feminine qal participle of zuv ("to flow"): "a flowing woman / a woman who flows"; LXX: γυνὴ ἥτις ἐὰν ῥέῃ αἷμα (gynē hētis ean rhēē haima, "a woman who flows blood"), using αἷμα (haima, "blood") — the LXX adds "blood" explicitly, while the MT has zo'vah (she-who-flows) without naming the substance; the MT names the substance in the next clause] is blood [dam] [dam — "blood"; the discharge is identified as blood; LXX has anticipated this; MT now names it] in her flesh [bi-vesarah] [bi-vesarah — "in her body / from her flesh"; LXX: ἐν τῷ σώματι αὐτῆς (en tō sōmati autēs, "in her body")] — she shall be [ve-hayetah] in her niddah [be-niddat'ah] [be-niddat'ah — "in her niddah / in her state of niddah [נִדָּה]"; niddah is the technical term for the menstrually separated woman; the root is uncertain — possibly from nadad ("to flee, to wander, to be excluded/separated") or from nidduy ("exclusion, banishment"); niddah denotes the state of separation that the menstruating woman enters: she is "separated" (like one excluded or set apart); LXX: ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς (en tē aphedrō autēs, "in her seat/separation"), using ἄφεδρος (aphedros, "privy, privy-place, menstrual separation") — the LXX term for niddah, suggesting the notion of "sitting apart"; in NT usage, ἄφεδρος refers to the digestive tract in Matt 15:17, but the Levitical usage is specific to menstrual separation] seven days [shivat yamim] [shivat yamim — "seven days"; the standard duration of the niddah state; LXX: ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας (hepta hēmeras, "seven days")] — and everyone who touches her [ve-khol-ha-nogea' bah] [ve-khol-ha-nogea' bah — "and everyone who touches her"; nogea' qal participle: "the one touching"; bah = "in her / her"; the contact with the niddah woman conveys tum'ah; LXX: πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος αὐτῆς (pas ho haptomenos autēs, "everyone touching her")] shall be unclean until the evening [yitma ad ha-erev]:

**20** "And everything [ve-khol] upon which she lies [asher tishkav alav] [asher tishkav alav — "upon which she lies"; the niddah's lying contaminates the surface; LXX: ἐφ' ᾧ ἂν κοιμηθῇ (eph' hō an koimēthē, "upon which she shall sleep")] during her niddah [be-niddat'ah] [be-niddat'ah — "in her niddah / during the period of her niddah"; LXX: ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς (en tē aphedrō autēs, "in her separation")] shall be unclean [yitma] — and everything upon which she sits [ve-khol asher teshev alav] [ve-khol asher teshev alav — "and everything upon which she sits"; the sitting-contamination parallel to v.4 for the zav; LXX: καὶ πᾶν ἐφ' ὃ ἂν καθίσῃ (kai pan eph' ho an kathisē, "and everything upon which she shall sit")] shall be unclean [yitma]:

**21** "And everyone who touches [ve-khol-ha-nogea'] her bed [be-mishkabah] [be-mishkabah — "her bed / her sleeping-place"; LXX: πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος τῆς κοίτης αὐτῆς (pas ho haptomenos tēs koitēs autēs, "everyone touching her bed")] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**22** "And everyone who touches [ve-khol-ha-nogea'] any implement [be-khol-ha-keli] [be-khol-ha-keli — "in / upon any implement / any object"; LXX: παντὸς σκεύους (pantos skeuos, "any vessel/implement")] upon which she has sat [asher-teshev alav] shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**23** "And if it is on the bed [ve-im al-ha-mishkav hu'] [ve-im al-ha-mishkav hu' — "and if upon the bed it is"; the grammatical subject is ambiguous — possibly referring to the object on the bed or to the discharge itself; the verse specifies: whether the person touches something that is on the niddah's bed (even if the woman herself is not present) or on any implement she has sat upon; LXX: εἴτε ἐπὶ τῆς κοίτης εἴτε ἐπὶ τοῦ σκεύους (eite epi tēs koitēs eite epi tou skeuos, "whether upon the bed or upon the implement")] or upon the implement [o al-ha-keli] [o al-ha-keli — "or upon the implement"] upon which she sits [asher-hi' yoshevet alav] [asher-hi' yoshevet alav — "upon which she sits / upon which she is sitting"; yoshevet = feminine qal participle of yashav; LXX: ἐφ' οὗ ἐὰν αὐτὴ καθίσῃ (eph' hou ean autē kathisē, "upon which she sits")] — when he touches [ba-neg'o-bo] [ba-neg'o-bo — "in his touching it / when he touches it"; infinitive construct of naga' with possessive suffix: "in his touch of it"; LXX: ἐν τῷ ἅπτεσθαι αὐτοῦ (en tō haptesthai autou, "in his touching it")] it [bo] he shall be unclean [yitma] until the evening [ad ha-erev]:

**24** "And if a man [ve-im shakhov yishkav ish] [ve-im shakhov yishkav ish — "and if, lying, a man lies"; the double infinitive-absolute construction: shakhov yishkav = emphatic, "if a man does indeed lie"; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κοιμηθεὶς κοιμηθῇ ἀνὴρ (ean de koimētheis koimēthē anēr, "if, sleeping, a man sleeps"), using the cognate-participle construction as in MT] with her [ittah] and her niddah is upon him [ve-tehi niddat'ah alav] [ve-tehi niddat'ah alav — "and her niddah is upon him"; the niddah-state attaches to the man who has intercourse with the niddah woman; alav = "upon him"; LXX: καὶ γένηται ἡ ἀφέδρος αὐτῆς ἐπ' αὐτόν (kai genētai hē aphedros autēs ep' auton, "and her menstrual separation comes upon him")] — he shall be unclean [ve-tame] seven days [shivat yamim] [shivat yamim — seven days; the man who has intercourse with the niddah woman takes on her full seven-day tum'ah, not merely the evening tum'ah of one who touches her; the duration reflects the seriousness of the contact] — and every bed [ve-khol-ha-mishkav] upon which he lies [asher yishkav alav] shall be unclean [yitma]:

---

**25** "And a woman [ve-ishah] when her discharge [ki-tizov] [ki-tizov — "when she flows / when her discharge flows"; zuv qal: "to flow"; the verb of the zov/zav root; LXX: ἐὰν ῥέῃ γυνή (ean rhēē gynē, "if a woman flows")] of blood [dam zo'vah] [dam zo'vah — "a discharge of blood / blood, she discharging"; dam = "blood"; zo'vah = feminine qal participle of zuv, "discharging, flowing woman"; the construction identifies this as a blood-discharge; LXX: ῥύσει αἵματος (rhysei haimatos, "with a flow of blood")] many days [yamim rabbim] [yamim rabbim — "many days"; the abnormal duration distinguishes the zavah from the niddah; while the niddah's seven-day period is normal and expected, the zavah's discharge extends beyond this normative period; LXX: ἡμέρας πλείους (hēmeras pleious, "more days")] not at the time of her niddah [be-lo' et niddat'ah] [be-lo' et niddat'ah — "not in the time of her niddah / beyond the time of her niddah"; the phrase defines abnormality: the discharge occurs outside the normal menstrual window; LXX: οὐκ ἐν τῷ καιρῷ τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς (ouk en tō kairō tēs aphedrou autēs, "not in the time of her separation")] or when she has a discharge beyond [ve-ki-tizov] [ve-ki-tizov — "or when she flows"; second circumstantial clause: another case is introduced; LXX: ἢ ἐὰν ῥέῃ (ē ean rhēē, "or if she flows")] her niddah [al niddat'ah] [al niddat'ah — "beyond her niddah / in excess of her niddah"; al = "upon, above, beyond"; the discharge continues past the seven-day period into additional days; LXX: μεθ' ἣν ἡμέραν ἂν ᾖ ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς (meth' hēn hēmeran an ē en tē aphedrō autēs, "beyond the day on which she is in her separation")] — all the days [kol-yemei] of her unclean discharge [zo'vah tum'at'ah] [zo'vah tum'at'ah — "her discharge of her impurity / the discharge of her impurity"; zo'vah ("her discharge") + tum'at'ah ("her impurity"): the two nouns in apposition define the zavah's condition; LXX: πᾶσαι αἱ ἡμέραι ῥύσεως ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς (pasai hai hēmerai rhyseōs akatharsias autēs, "all the days of the flow of her impurity")] shall be as the days of her niddah [ki-yemei niddat'ah] [ki-yemei niddat'ah — "as the days of her niddah"; the zavah's abnormal-discharge days are classified under the same niddah-impurity framework; LXX: ὡς αἱ ἡμέραι τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς (hōs hai hēmerai tēs aphedrou autēs, "as the days of her separation")] — she shall be unclean [tehyeh tameh] [tehyeh tameh — "she shall be unclean"; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται (akathartos estai, "she shall be unclean")]:

**26** "Every bed [kol-ha-mishkav] upon which she lies [asher tishkav alav] all the days of her discharge [kol-yemei zo'vah] [kol-yemei zo'vah — "all the days of her discharge"; throughout the duration of the zavah's abnormal flow; LXX: πᾶσα κοίτη ἐφ' ἧς ἂν κοιμηθῇ πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας τῆς ῥύσεως αὐτῆς (pasa koitē eph' hēs an koimēthē pasas tas hēmeras tēs rhyseōs autēs, "every bed upon which she lies all the days of her discharge")] shall be to her as the bed of her niddah [ke-mishkav niddat'ah yihyeh-lah] [ke-mishkav niddat'ah yihyeh-lah — "shall be to her as the bed of her niddah / shall be for her as the bed of her menstrual separation"; the zavah's sleeping-objects have the same status as the niddah's; LXX: ὡς ἡ κοίτη τῆς ἀφέδρου (hōs hē koitē tēs aphedrou, "as the bed of her separation")] — and every implement [ve-khol-ha-keli] upon which she sits [asher-teshev alav] shall be unclean [yitma] as the uncleanness of her niddah [ke-tum'at niddat'ah] [ke-tum'at niddat'ah — "as the uncleanness of her niddah"; the impurity-level is equated with normal menstrual tum'ah; LXX: κατὰ τὴν ἀκαθαρσίαν τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς (kata tēn akatharsian tēs aphedrou autēs, "according to the uncleanness of her separation")]:

**27** "And everyone [ve-khol] who touches [ha-nogea'] them [bam] [bam — "in them / upon them"; referring to the bed and implements of vv.25–26; LXX: πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος αὐτῶν (pas ho haptomenos autōn, "everyone touching them")] shall be unclean [yitma] — he shall wash his clothes and shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening:

**28** "And if she is cleansed [ve-im taher'ah] [ve-im taher'ah — "and if she is cleansed / if she becomes clean"; taher qal perfect 3fs: "she becomes clean / she has become clean"; the condition: the abnormal discharge has ceased; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ καθαρισθῇ (ean de katharisthē, "and if she is cleansed")] from her discharge [mi-zo'vah] [mi-zo'vah — "from her discharge"; the cessation of the zov; LXX: ἀπὸ τῆς ῥύσεως αὐτῆς (apo tēs rhyseōs autēs, "from her discharge")] she shall count [ve-safrah-lah] for herself [lah] [ve-safrah-lah — "and she shall count for herself"; the reflexive lah added as with the zav in v.13; she counts seven clean days after the discharge stops; LXX: καὶ ἐξαριθμήσεται ἑαυτῇ (kai exarithmēsetai heautē, "and she shall count for herself")] seven days [shivat yamim] [shivat yamim — "seven days"; the seven-day clean count mirrors that of the zav (v.13); LXX: ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας (hepta hēmeras, "seven days")] — and afterward [ve-achar] she shall be clean [tit'har] [tit'har — "she shall be clean"; hithpa'el of taher: "shall become clean / shall purify herself"; LXX: καθαρισθήσεται (katharisthēsetai, "shall be cleansed")]:

**29** "And on the eighth day [u-va-yom ha-shemini] she shall take [tiqqach-lah] for herself [lah] [tiqqach-lah — "she shall take for herself"; feminine form of the verb; lah = "for herself"; LXX: καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ λήμψεται ἑαυτῇ (kai tē hēmera tē ogdoē lēmpsetai heautē, "and on the eighth day she shall take for herself")] two turtledoves [shtei torim] or two young pigeons [o shnei benei yonah] [the same two-bird offering as the zav in v.14; the parallel structure of vv.14–15 and vv.29–30 is exact: both the zav and the zavah bring identical offerings; the gender parity in the offering requirement is notable; LXX: δύο τρυγόνας ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν (dyo trygones ē dyo neossous peristerōn, "two turtledoves or two young pigeons")] and shall bring them [ve-hevi'ah otam] to the priest [el-ha-kohen] to the entrance [el-petach] of the tent of meeting [ohel mo'ed]:

**30** "And the priest shall make [ve-'asah ha-kohen] one as a sin offering [et-ha-echad chattat] and one as a burnt offering [ve-et-ha-echad olah] [the same chattat/olah pairing as v.15; LXX: καὶ ποιήσει ὁ ἱερεὺς τὸ ἓν περὶ ἁμαρτίας καὶ τὸ ἓν εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα (kai poiēsei ho hiereus to hen peri hamartias kai to hen eis holokautōma, "and the priest shall make the one as a sin offering and the one as a burnt offering")] — and the priest shall make atonement [ve-khiper alav ha-kohen] [ve-khiper alav ha-kohen — "and the priest shall make atonement over/for her"; the masculine alav here covers her — a grammatical masculine default for the third-person general atonement formula; some MSS read aleha (feminine); LXX: καὶ ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτῆς ὁ ἱερεύς (kai exilasetai peri autēs ho hiereus, "and the priest shall make expiation for her") — LXX uses the feminine αὐτῆς correctly] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH] for her unclean discharge [me-zo'vat tum'at'ah] [me-zo'vat tum'at'ah — "from her discharge of her uncleanness / on account of her unclean discharge"; zo'vah + tum'ah together, as in v.25; LXX: ἀπὸ ῥύσεως ἀκαθαρσίας αὐτῆς (apo rhyseōs akatharsias autēs, "from the discharge of her uncleanness")]:

---

**31** "And you shall separate [ve-hizartem] [ve-hizartem — "and you shall keep separate / you shall cause to be separated"; hiphil of nazar ("to dedicate, to separate, to abstain, to set apart"): in the hiphil, "to cause to be separate / to keep at a distance / to hold separate"; the root of nazir (Nazirite) — the person consecrated by separation; here the verb addresses the entire community's responsibility to maintain the separations legislated in this chapter; the subject is the priestly/legislative leadership (Moshe and Aharon of v.1) charged with ensuring the people observe these separations; LXX: καὶ ἐκτενεῖτε (kai ekteneite, "and you shall send forth/keep away"), using ἐκτείνω (ekteinō, "to stretch out, to keep at distance") — a slightly different image but conveying the separation; many LXX MSS read ἀφοριεῖτε (aphoriereite, "shall set apart") instead, which is a more direct equivalent of hizartem] the sons of Yisra'el [et-benei Yisra'el] from their uncleanness [mi-tum'atam] [mi-tum'atam — "from their impurities"; the object of hizartem: the people must be separated FROM their tum'ah — either by observing the separation-regulations of this chapter, or by the priests' teaching enabling the people to avoid aggravating their impurity; LXX: τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀκαθαρσιῶν αὐτῶν (tous huious Israēl apo tōn akatharsion autōn, "the sons of Israel from their impurities")] — so that they do not die [ve-lo' yamutu] [ve-lo' yamutu — "and they shall not die"; the purpose clause: the motivation for all of this legislation is life; failure to observe the separations results in death; the specific mechanism: the tameh person defiles the Tabernacle, and the divine Presence in the defiled Tabernacle destroys them; LXX: καὶ οὐκ ἀποθανοῦνται (kai ouk apothanountai, "and they shall not die")] in their uncleanness [be-tum'atam] [be-tum'atam — "in their impurity / because of their impurity"; their uncleanness is what kills them, because it defiles the Tabernacle; LXX: ἐν τῇ ἀκαθαρσίᾳ αὐτῶν (en tē akatharsia autōn, "in their impurity")] when they defile [be-tame'am] [be-tame'am — "in their defiling / when they defile"; infinitive construct of tame' with possessive prefix be- and suffix -am ("their"): "in their making-unclean"; LXX: ἐν τῷ μιαίνειν αὐτούς (en tō miainein autous, "in their defiling")] my tabernacle [et-mishkani] [et-mishkani — "my Tabernacle / my dwelling-place"; mishkan [מִשְׁכָּן] from shakhan ("to dwell"): the portable sanctuary, the Tabernacle; the first-person possessive: "my dwelling-place" — the LORD's; this is the theological center of the entire chapter: the Tabernacle is the LORD's dwelling, and its defilement by the tameh is what the legislation is designed to prevent; LXX: τὴν σκηνήν μου (tēn skēnēn mou, "my tent/tabernacle"), using σκηνή (skēnē, "tent, tabernacle")] that is in their midst [asher be-tokham] [asher be-tokham — "which is in their midst / which is among them"; the Tabernacle is not remote but dwells among the people — be-tokham ("in their midst") — which is precisely what makes defilement so dangerous; the holy is near; LXX: τὴν οὖσαν ἐν αὐτοῖς (tēn ousan en autois, "which is among them")]:

**32** "This [zot] is the instruction [ha-torah] [ha-torah — "the instruction / the law"; torah as always: the divine teaching/instruction, not merely legal code; LXX: οὗτος ὁ νόμος (houtos ho nomos, "this is the law")] of the one who has a discharge [la-zav] [la-zav — "of the zav / for the zav"; the legislation is summarized beginning with the zav; LXX: τοῦ γονορρυοῦς (tou gonorrhyous, "of the one with a discharge")] and of the one from whom goes out [u-le-asher teze mimenu] [u-le-asher teze mimenu — "and of the one from whom goes out / and for the one who has an emission"; the seminal emission of vv.16–18; LXX: καὶ οὗ ἂν ἐξέλθῃ ἐξ αὐτοῦ (kai hou an exelthē ex autou, "and of whoever goes out from him")] an emission of seed [shkhvat-zera] [shkhvat-zera — "emission of seed / seminal emission"; as in v.16; LXX: σπέρματος κοίτη (spermatos koitē, "a lying of seed")] to become unclean [le-tame'ah-bo] [le-tame'ah-bo — "to become unclean by it / for being made unclean by it"; infinitive construct of tame' with preposition le- and suffix -bo ("by it"): the emission makes him tameh; LXX: ὥστε μιαίνεσθαι ἐν αὐτῇ (hōste miainesthai en autē, "so as to be defiled by it")]:

**33** "And of the woman in her niddah [ve-ha-davah be-niddat'ah] [ve-ha-davah be-niddat'ah — "and of the one ailing / the weak one in her niddah"; ha-davah = "the one who is ill / the sick one / the ailing one"; davah from davah ("to be sick, to be ill, to be weak") — often used in the context of menstruation as a time of weakness or pain; niddat'ah = "her niddah"; the term ha-davah is used specifically for the menstruating woman as one in a state of physical/ritual weakness; this differs from the niddah (which is more of a status-term) by emphasizing the physical dimension of the condition; LXX: τῆς ἀδιαθέτου ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς (tēs adiathetou en tē aphedrō autēs, "of the one indisposed in her separation"), using ἀδιάθετος (adiathetous, "indisposed, unwell, one whose condition is disordered") — a rare LXX term capturing the davah's weakness-dimension] and of the one having a discharge [ve-la-zav] [ve-la-zav — "and of the one having a discharge"; referring to the zav as a summarizing closure; LXX: καὶ τοῦ γονορρυοῦς (kai tou gonorrhyous, "and of the one having a discharge")] — of the male [la-zakhar] [la-zakhar — "for the male / concerning the male"; zakhar = "male"; the summary specifies gender; LXX: τῷ ἄρσενι (tō arseni, "to the male"), using ἄρσην (arsēn, "male")] and of the female [ve-la-neqevah] [ve-la-neqevah — "and of the female / concerning the female"; neqevah = "female"; LXX: καὶ τῷ θήλει (kai tō thēlei, "and to the female"), using θῆλυς (thēlys, "female")] — and of the man [ve-la-ish] who lies [asher yishkav] with an unclean woman [im-temei'ah] [im-temei'ah — "with the unclean one / with a tameh woman"; temei'ah = feminine form of tameh; the man who lies with the niddah woman of v.24; LXX: καὶ τοῦ κοιμωμένου μετ' ἀκαθάρτου (kai tou koimōmenou met' akathartou, "and of the one sleeping with an unclean woman")]:

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67r–67v:** The text of Leviticus 15 occupies both sides of folio 67. The Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks are present throughout, enabling precise identification of all technical terms and their grammatical forms. Paragraph divisions (parashot) are visible at the major structural breaks: before v.1 (the chapter opening with the dual Moshe-Aharon address), before v.16 (the transition from abnormal to normal male discharge), before v.19 (the transition to female legislation), and before v.25 (the abnormal female discharge). These paragraph markers in the Leningrad Codex align with the four-part symmetrical structure of the chapter. The hapax and rare terms — zo'vah (vv.19, 25, 28, 30), ha-davah (v.33), and the phrase be-lo' et niddat'ah (v.25) — are all clearly vocalized with full Tiberian pointing, supporting the traditional readings. The technical compound shkhvat-zera (vv.16, 17, 18, 32) is consistently written as a construct-chain in the codex.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 101–102:** The Vaticanus LXX text of ch.15 runs across these two pages in continuous uncial script. The Greek presents a close and generally reliable translation of the Hebrew, with the consistent rendering of zav/zavah as γονορρυής (gonorrhyēs, "one with a flux") and niddah as ἄφεδρος (aphedros). Notable is the LXX's explicit insertion of αἷμα ("blood") in v.19 where the MT has only zo'vah ("she who flows"), and the LXX's feminine pronoun αὐτῆς in v.30 for the atonement formula where MT reads the masculine alav. No significant lacunae are visible in the manuscript pages.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **zav / zov** (vv.2–15, 32–33): The male subject of abnormal discharge. Zav [זָב] (the man) preserved with gloss at first occurrence; zov [זוֹב] (the discharge) similarly preserved. The root zuv ("to flow") is consistently reflected. The LXX uses γονορρυής (gonorrhyēs, "one with a genital flow"), from which "gonorrhea" derives etymologically, though the biblical condition is not necessarily identical to modern gonorrhea — it likely encompasses any persistent, abnormal male genital discharge.

2. **shkhvat-zera** (vv.16, 17, 18, 32): Rendered "emission of seed" throughout, preserving the literal construct: "lying of seed." The Hebrew treats the seminal emission as a normal physiological event (unlike the abnormal zov) that creates a lighter, short-duration tum'ah resolved by bathing and laundering until evening. No sin is implied; the legislation manages the ritual dimension of bodily life.

3. **niddah** (vv.19, 20, 24, 25, 26, 33): Preserved as a technical term with gloss at first occurrence. The root connects to concepts of separation/exclusion. The niddah period is seven days (v.19) — the duration of the normal menstrual cycle in ancient reckoning. The niddah is not sinful or shameful in this legislation; she is in a state requiring management for the sake of sanctuary access, and the chapter provides the resolution.

4. **zavah** (vv.25–30): The female with abnormal discharge — the female counterpart to the zav. While the MT uses the term zo'vah (feminine participle of zuv) rather than the noun zavah, the traditional technical term is zavah [זָבָה]. Rendered as "the woman with a discharge / the zavah" in the notes; the translation body preserves the participial phrasing of the MT.

5. **dam rav** (not explicitly present as a fixed phrase but implied in vv.25): The phrase yamim rabbim ("many days") combined with the blood-discharge of v.25 establishes the abnormality. The "many days" beyond the niddah period, and "a discharge beyond her niddah" (al niddat'ah, v.25) — both define the zavah's condition as exceeding the normative seven-day window.

6. **ve-hizartem** (v.31): "And you shall separate" — from the root nazar, the same root as nazir (Nazirite). The instruction to "keep the sons of Yisra'el separate from their impurities" is the theological summary of the entire chapter. The priests/leadership are charged with maintaining the separation on behalf of the community. The consequence of failure is death by defilement of the Tabernacle.

7. **mishkani** (v.31): "My Tabernacle" — the first-person divine possessive is prominent: mishkani = "my dwelling-place." The Tabernacle belongs to the LORD. Defilement of it is an offense against the divine Presence itself. This possessive frames the entire chapter's legislation as protecting something sacred and personal to YHWH.

8. **ha-davah** (v.33): "The ailing one / the sick one" — davah is an unusual word used for the niddah woman emphasizing her physical state of weakness or illness during menstruation. It is not merely a synonym for niddah but adds a dimension of bodily vulnerability. The LXX's ἀδιάθετος ("one who is indisposed, unwell") captures this nuance. The closing summary chooses this word to encompass the niddah, perhaps to emphasize the human-bodily dimension of the entire chapter's legislation.

9. **The symmetry of zav / zavah**: The chapter is deliberately constructed as four parallel units with male/female parity. The abnormal male discharge (vv.2–15) and the abnormal female discharge (vv.25–30) use the same structure: a definition of the condition, transmission rules, and an eight-day resolution with two birds. The normal male emission (vv.16–18) and the normal female discharge (vv.19–24) are shorter sections using the same resolution structure (bathing/laundering until evening). This four-part symmetry is a literary and theological achievement: the chapter treats male and female bodily life with structural equality.

10. **ve-lo' yamutu be-tum'atam** (v.31): "So that they do not die in their uncleanness" — the explicit purpose-statement of the legislation. The danger is not primarily hygienic or moral but theological: the tameh person who approaches or defiles the Tabernacle, where the living God dwells (be-tokham, "in their midst"), faces death. The regulations of ch.15 are thus life-giving instructions: they provide the way to manage impurity so that Israel can continue to live in the presence of the holy God.

11. **Contact-transmission gradations**: The chapter encodes a nuanced hierarchy of transmitted tum'ah. Direct contact with the zav (v.7) or sitting on his implements (v.6) transmits the standard laundering-bathing-until-evening tum'ah. Lying in a house where a tameh-object was (v.23) conveys the same. Intercourse with the niddah woman (v.24) transmits a heavier seven-day tum'ah. The gradation reflects the intimacy and duration of contact.

12. **Hechtim vs. rur** (v.3): The MT of v.3 distinguishes between the zov that is actively flowing (rur, "flows") and the zov that has been retained/stopped (hechtim, "has retained"). Both states render the man tameh. The LXX reads this as two temporal stages: flowing and ceasing. The MT's hechtim (from chatam, "to seal") suggests a different scenario: the discharge is blocked internally but the condition persists. Both readings are ancient; the MT's reading is preserved in the translation.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX Reading | Nature of Divergence |
|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| 3 | besaro rur ... o hechtim, "his flesh flows ... or has retained" | εἴτε ῥεῖ ... εἴτε ἐπιλέλοιπεν, "whether flows ... or has ceased" | LXX reads hechtim as "ceased" (cessation of flow) rather than "retained" — a different understanding of the condition |
| 11 | ve-yadav lo' shafal be-mayim, "and his hands he has not rinsed in water" | αἱ χεῖρες αὐτοῦ οὐ νένιπται ὕδατι, "his hands have not been washed in water" | LXX uses νίπτω (niptō, "to wash hands") rather than a flooding/rinsing verb — preserves the handwashing sense; close to MT |
| 19 | ki-tihyeh zo'vah dam bi-vesarah, "when she discharges blood from her body" | ἐὰν ῥέῃ αἷμα, "if she flows blood" | LXX adds αἷμα ("blood") explicitly at the start, before the MT names the substance; MT presents zo'vah first, then dam — LXX reorders |
| 25 | be-lo' et niddat'ah o ki-tizov al niddat'ah, "not at the time of her niddah or when she flows beyond her niddah" | οὐκ ἐν τῷ καιρῷ τῆς ἀφέδρου... μεθ' ἣν ἡμέραν ἂν ᾖ, "not in the time of her separation... beyond the day on which she is in her separation" | LXX is a paraphrase, capturing the two scenarios (outside normal timing; beyond normal duration) but with different grammatical structure |
| 30 | ve-khiper alav ha-kohen (masculine alav), "the priest shall atone over him" | ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτῆς (feminine αὐτῆς), "shall make expiation for her" | LXX corrects the grammatical anomaly of MT's masculine alav by using the feminine αὐτῆς — the subject is clearly the zavah; MT may use masculine as a general atonement formula |
| 31 | ve-hizartem et-benei Yisra'el mi-tum'atam, "you shall separate the sons of Israel from their impurities" | καὶ ἐκτενεῖτε τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀκαθαρσιῶν αὐτῶν, "and you shall keep the sons of Israel away from their impurities" | LXX uses ἐκτείνω ("to stretch out, to extend away") for hizartem; other LXX MSS read ἀφορίζω ("to set apart") — the LXX transmission shows uncertainty in rendering this theologically dense verb |
| 33 | ha-davah be-niddat'ah, "the ailing one in her niddah" | τῆς ἀδιαθέτου ἐν τῇ ἀφέδρῳ αὐτῆς, "the one indisposed in her separation" | LXX renders ha-davah with ἀδιάθετος ("indisposed, unwell") — a rare term capturing the illness/weakness dimension of davah; a good equivalent |
| 32–33 | Summary lists zav, shkhvat-zera, niddah/davah, zavah | LXX summary is slightly compressed: omits explicit mention of zavah as a separate category, subsuming under general "discharge" | Minor LXX compression in the colophon summary; MT is more exhaustive in listing each category |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|----------------|---------------|-------|
| זָב | zav | "zav" (with gloss) | The male with abnormal genital discharge; root zuv ("to flow") |
| זוֹב | zov | "discharge / zov" | The discharge itself; preserved at first occurrence, rendered "discharge" in running text |
| שִׁכְבַת-זֶרַע | shkhvat-zera | "emission of seed" | Construct: "lying of seed"; the normal seminal emission; preserved at first occurrence |
| נִדָּה | niddah | "niddah" (with gloss) | The menstrually separated woman; preserved throughout as a technical term |
| זָבָה / זֹבָה | zavah / zo'vah | "one with a discharge / zavah" | The woman with abnormal flow; feminine counterpart of zav |
| דָּוָה | davah | "the ailing one" | Used in v.33 for the niddah woman, emphasizing physical weakness |
| טָמֵא / טָמְאָה | tameh / tam'ah | "unclean" | Standard rendering throughout |
| טָהוֹר | tahor | "clean" | Standard rendering |
| תּוּמְאָה | tum'ah | "uncleanness / impurity" | The state of ritual impurity |
| טָהֳרָה | taharah | "cleansing / purification" | The process of restoring clean status |
| מִשְׁכָּב | mishkav | "bed / sleeping-surface" | From shakav ("to lie down") |
| רָחַץ | rachatz | "bathe / bathe in water" | Bathing the body; distinct from kibbes (laundering) |
| כִּבֶּס | kibbes | "launder / wash his clothes" | Piel: thorough washing of garments |
| חַטָּאת | chattat | "sin offering" | Standard rendering |
| עֹלָה | olah | "burnt offering" | Standard rendering |
| יהוה | YHWH | "the LORD" | Standard rendering |
| מִשְׁכָּן | mishkan | "Tabernacle / dwelling-place" | The LORD's portable sanctuary; first-person possessive mishkani = "my Tabernacle" |
| כִּפֶּר | kipper | "make atonement" | Piel of kapar; the central Levitical atonement verb |
| תּוֹרָה | torah | "instruction / law" | v.32: "this is the instruction of the zav" |
| מַיִם חַיִּים | mayim chayyim | "living water" | Fresh running/spring water; v.13 |
| סָפַר | safar | "count" | The counting of clean days; vv.13, 28 |
